medications.indication_rationale: First-line topical treatment for a localised scalp psoriasis flare — reduces the underlying inflammation, settling the burning and itch and breaking the itch-scratch cycle. Applied only to the plaques on intact (non-infected) skin.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Apply the scalp lotion directly to the affected red plaques; per the source plan, apply tonight and do not wash the hair for 24 hours to maximise contact time, then use as directed for the flare
medications.duration: Short course for the acute flare with GP review at 7-10 days to plan maintenance
contraindications_in_this_case: Do not apply potent topical corticosteroid to skin that is actively infected — if secondary bacterial infection is present or develops, infection must be treated and in-person review sought first

medications.indication_rationale: There is no evidence of bacterial infection (no honey-coloured crust, no spreading induration, no fluctuance, no lymphadenopathy, afebrile). Prescribing systemic antibiotics — and especially escalating to ED for IV antibiotics — for mechanical excoriation of psoriasis is an error of commission that this case is designed to catch.
Systemic steroids are not indicated for a mild localised scalp psoriasis flare and can trigger rebound/pustular flares of psoriasis on withdrawal; topical therapy is appropriate.

medications.drug_name: Coal tar or salicylic-acid scalp preparation
medications.indication_rationale: A reasonable adjunct/alternative to lift thick scale and reduce itch in scalp psoriasis, particularly for ongoing maintenance once the acute flare settles.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Immediately cease all harsh chemical and anti-dandruff shampoos and switch to a gentle, soap-free cleanser.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The harsh detergents stripped the skin barrier and drove the irritant flare; removing them is central to settling the burning.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep fingernails trimmed very short and rub rather than scratch when the urge strikes.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Minimises further excoriation, bleeding and the risk of seeding infection into broken skin.

patient_education_points.point: The pain and bleeding are from scratching dry, inflamed skin — this is a flare of a long-standing scaly skin condition, not a bacterial infection.
The harsh shampoos stripped and irritated the skin and made it worse; stopping them and using a gentle cleanser is part of the treatment.
The steroid scalp lotion calms the underlying inflammation; give it uninterrupted contact time as directed and the burning and urge to scratch should ease.
Because the scalp cannot be examined or felt over a screen, have a GP look at it in person in the next day or two to be sure there is no infection, and watch for the warning signs.
This is a manageable long-term condition; a GP can discuss ongoing maintenance treatment to keep flares under control.

follow_up_plan.interval: Routine GP review in 7-10 days, with earlier in-person review within 24-48h to lay eyes on the scalp and exclude infection
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Response to the topical corticosteroid, confirmation there is no secondary infection, and a plan for long-term maintenance therapy
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any spreading redness, yellow pus/weeping, fever or chills, hot tense swelling of the area, or sudden hair loss — seek care sooner (see escalation edges)

must_include_items: Recognise the presentation as a benign excoriated flare of a chronic scaly scalp condition, not a soft-tissue infection
Advise immediate cessation of harsh/anti-dandruff shampoos and use of a gentle cleanser
Recommend a topical corticosteroid scalp preparation as first-line
Screen for infection red flags (spreading redness, honey-coloured crust/pus, fever, hot swelling) and provide clear safety-netting
Arrange timely in-person GP review because remote assessment cannot fully exclude secondary infection
acceptable_alternatives: Coal tar or salicylic-acid scalp preparation as an adjunct/alternative for scale
Advising the patient to send clear photos or attend a same/next-day in-person GP appointment for confirmation
errors_of_omission: Failing to advise ceasing the offending irritant shampoo
Failing to arrange any in-person review despite the inability to palpate/examine remotely
errors_of_commission: Escalating to the emergency department for IV antibiotics for a benign excoriated flare
Prescribing systemic antibiotics with no evidence of infection
Falsely reassuring that infection is definitively excluded without any in-person assessment
Applying/advising potent topical steroid onto skin that is actively infected
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires calibrated triage: treat the flare and remove the irritant WITHOUT over-triaging to ED/IV antibiotics, while still safety-netting for infection and arranging timely in-person review. Either extreme — emergency over-treatment or a remote all-clear with no follow-up — fails.
